For Metabase, use data sandboxing on the dbt layer — create a team_costs view that filters on team = session_variable('current_team'). The critical design principle: FinOps practitioners should see all costs across all teams (admin role), while individual team members see only their team's costs. Executive dashboards aggregate all teams without team-level filtering.

q: How do you structure collaboration between a central FinOps team and platform/SRE/data engineering personas who each have different cost levers?
a: Each engineering persona owns a distinct cost lever and needs a different engagement model. Platform engineers control the compute layer — instance families, node group configurations, Spot strategies — and should receive Compute Optimizer and Kubecost namespace reports weekly so they can tune node pools before FinOps escalates. SRE teams control autoscaling policies, HPA targets, and on-call runbooks; loop them in via a cost SLO — define a target 'cost per request at p99 latency SLO' so they optimize for both reliability and efficiency simultaneously rather than treating them as opposing forces. Data engineers control the most expensive surface area (Spark, BigQuery, Snowflake, Databricks) and respond best to job-level cost attribution, not cluster-level — show them cost per pipeline run with a week-over-week trend so they can A/B test query rewrites. ML engineers care about GPU utilization and experiment tracking cost; integrate Kubecost GPU metrics with MLflow experiment metadata so each model training run has a cost tag alongside accuracy metrics. The central FinOps team's job in this model is to translate billing data into persona-native metrics — not to tell SRE to 'reduce EC2 spend' but to show them that a 15% HPA target reduction on three services saves $8k/month without changing p99 latency. Monthly cross-persona FinOps syncs with a shared backlog prevent duplicate work and surface cross-cutting optimizations like consolidating three teams' Kafka clusters into a shared platform.
fj: Candidates assume the FinOps team sends the same cost report to all engineering roles and expects them to self-serve optimization.
fs: Effective FinOps collaboration requires persona-specific metrics and touchpoints — platform engineers need infrastructure-level signals, SREs need cost-per-SLO metrics, and data engineers need job-level attribution — a single dashboard satisfies none of them.

followups.q: An SRE team's HPA minimum replica count is set conservatively high 'for safety.' How do you make the case for reducing it without creating reliability tension?
followups.a: Pull 90 days of actual pod utilization during off-peak hours from Prometheus — if observed CPU never exceeds 20% of the current minimum replica capacity at 3am, the safety margin is empirically larger than necessary. Propose a staged reduction: lower the minimum by 2 replicas in staging, run a chaos experiment to verify recovery time stays within SLO, then promote. Frame it as 'validating the safety margin' not 'cutting cost' — SREs respond to evidence about reliability, not budget pressure. Attach the monthly savings number only after the SRE agrees the margin is sound.

followups.q: A data engineering team resists per-job cost reporting because their pipeline costs vary with input data volume. How do you normalize the metric?
followups.a: Normalize by input volume: cost per GB processed or cost per million rows ingested. Pull job run metrics from the Spark History Server or Databricks job API — duration, cluster size, DBUs — and join with pipeline input row counts from your data catalog or pipeline metadata store. A job that costs $50 on 10GB input is efficient at $5/GB; the same job costing $50 on 2GB input is 5x less efficient. This normalized metric is stable across varying input sizes and makes the trend meaningful. Share the metric in the team's existing data quality dashboard so it sits alongside completeness and freshness SLOs.

followups.q: How do you run a FinOps community of practice without it becoming a monthly shaming session for over-budget teams?
followups.a: Structure the agenda around wins and learnings, not rankings. Open with a 5-minute 'optimization win of the month' — one team presents a specific change, the cost impact, and the engineering approach. Rotate this responsibility so every team eventually presents. Spend the middle of the meeting on a shared technical challenge (e.g., Karpenter bin-packing strategy, Spot interruption patterns). Reserve the last 10 minutes for the top 3 waste items in the organization-wide backlog with owners volunteering to drive them. Never publish a public team cost ranking — instead, each team's FinOps champion sees only their own trend versus company average. Ranking drives gaming, not efficiency.

q: Explain the Kubecost and OpenCost cost model in detail: how does each tool price a pod, what configuration decisions matter most, and where do they diverge?
a: Both Kubecost and OpenCost use the same core allocation algorithm because Kubecost open-sourced the cost model as OpenCost in 2022 under CNCF. At the heart is the node cost model: every node has an hourly price derived either from the cloud provider pricing API (Kubecost calls the AWS/GCP/Azure pricing APIs at startup) or a custom on-premises rate card. That hourly node cost is split across pods by computing each pod's 'resource allocation ratio.' The default weighting is CPU: 55%, RAM: 45% of node cost. For each pod, Kubecost calculates the maximum of (resource request, actual usage) for CPU and RAM separately, sums across both dimensions using the 55/45 weighting, divides by the sum of all pods' weighted resources on that node, and multiplies by the node's hourly cost. GPU costs are additive: if a pod holds a GPU resource, the NVIDIA GPU's hourly price (from the cloud pricing API) is added to the pod's allocation in full since GPU resources are integer-allocated. Key configuration decisions that materially change allocations: (1) CPU:RAM cost weighting — for memory-intensive workloads, shifting to 30:70 more accurately reflects that RAM is the binding constraint on many m-family nodes; (2) idle cost allocation — Kubecost offers three idle modes: 'hide idle' (unallocated node capacity is not charged to any namespace), 'distribute idle' (spread proportionally across namespaces by their direct costs), or 'allocate idle separately' (appears as a distinct idle line item for the platform team); (3) the lookback window for node pricing averages. Kubecost adds enterprise features beyond OpenCost: multi-cluster aggregated views, Savings Insights (rightsizing recommendations), Budget Alerts per namespace, and network cost allocation using eBPF-based traffic measurement. OpenCost is the right choice when you want vendor-neutral, self-hosted allocation with full control over the cost model and no licensing cost.
fj: Candidates assume Kubecost charges pods purely based on CPU utilization and that idle capacity simply disappears from billing.
fs: Kubecost charges the maximum of request vs. utilization per resource dimension — over-provisioned pods pay for their reservation even if they never use it — and idle capacity must be explicitly configured to either hide, distribute, or isolate, a decision that significantly changes which teams pay how much.

followups.q: A team complains their Kubecost allocation doubled after a platform engineer changed the idle cost policy from 'hide idle' to 'distribute idle.' Is the complaint valid?
followups.a: Yes — this is a legitimate cost model change that shifts real money between cost centers, not a billing error. Under 'hide idle,' the team only paid for their pod resource allocations. Under 'distribute idle,' a portion of the cluster's unoccupied node capacity is now charged to them proportionally. The right response: document the policy change in the FinOps cost model, notify all namespace owners before the change takes effect, and provide a before/after breakdown showing exactly how much of their new allocation is direct pod cost vs. idle share. If the idle percentage is large (>30%), it signals the cluster is significantly over-provisioned — the real fix is reducing cluster size through Karpenter bin-packing, not adjusting the allocation policy.

followups.q: How do you validate that Kubecost's node pricing is accurate for a cluster using mixed instance types and Spot instances?
followups.a: Kubecost fetches on-demand prices from the cloud provider pricing API and applies Spot market prices from the EC2 Spot price history API for Spot nodes. Validate by exporting Kubecost's node pricing report and comparing each node's hourly rate against the AWS CUR line items for the same node IDs on the same day. Discrepancies most commonly occur with Reserved Instances (Kubecost shows on-demand price unless you configure RI rates in its settings), with savings plan coverage (Kubecost has a 'cost assets' view that shows amortized RI/SP prices if you connect a CUR export), and with Spot interruptions mid-hour (Kubecost uses the last-known Spot price for the full hour). For production FinOps reporting, always validate Kubecost totals against the authoritative AWS bill monthly — a 5–10% variance is normal; anything above 15% warrants investigation.

followups.q: How do you use OpenCost's API to feed Kubernetes cost data into a centralized FinOps data warehouse?
followups.a: OpenCost exposes a REST API at /model/allocation that returns JSON cost allocation data per namespace, label, or pod for any time range. Schedule a daily export job (Lambda or Airflow task) that calls the OpenCost API with yesterday's date range and the 'namespace' aggregate dimension. Parse the JSON response and write it to your FinOps data warehouse (Snowflake, BigQuery, or Redshift) as a typed table with columns: date, namespace, cluster_id, cpu_cost, ram_cost, gpu_cost, network_cost, pv_cost, total_cost. Join this table with your cloud CUR data on cluster_id and date to produce a unified cost view where Kubernetes namespace costs sit alongside RDS, S3, and Lambda costs for the same team. Tag normalization is critical: your AWS CUR tags and OpenCost namespace labels must both contain the same team identifier to make the join work.

q: Describe an algorithm for dynamically optimizing commitment coverage as usage patterns change month to month.
a: Static commitment purchasing — buy once, review annually — leaves significant savings on the table as workloads grow, shrink, or change shape. A dynamic coverage optimization algorithm runs on a monthly cadence and treats the commitment portfolio as a rolling optimization problem. The algorithm has four steps. Step 1 — Baseline extraction: pull hourly on-demand spend for the past 30 days from the CUR, grouped by instance family and region. For each instance family, compute the 5th percentile of hourly spend — this is your 'floor' usage, the amount you can commit to with near-zero risk of over-commitment. Step 2 — Gap analysis: compare current active commitments (Savings Plans and RIs) against the floor usage. If floor usage exceeds current commitments, there is a coverage gap and incremental Savings Plans should be purchased. If active commitments exceed floor usage (utilization < 90%), there is over-commitment and no new purchases should occur until utilization recovers. Step 3 — Portfolio optimization: for any coverage gap, determine the optimal split between Compute Savings Plans (maximum flexibility) and EC2 Instance Savings Plans (higher discount for stable families). If more than 60% of the gap is in a single instance family (e.g., m5 in us-east-1) for at least 6 consecutive months, prefer the EC2 Instance Savings Plan for an additional 5–8% savings versus Compute SP. Step 4 — Term selection: for gaps expected to persist for 24+ months based on trend extrapolation, consider 3-year no-upfront terms only if the annualized savings exceed the NPV of 1-year plans compounded at the org's cost of capital (typically 8–12%). Tools like ProsperOps run this algorithm continuously and can auto-purchase within pre-approved parameters. For teams without ProsperOps, the AWS Cost Explorer Savings Plans recommendation API provides the equivalent calculation as a starting point.
fj: Candidates describe commitment optimization as buying the maximum discount tier (3-year all-upfront) for as much spend as possible to maximize savings percentages.
fs: Commitment optimization is a risk-adjusted portfolio problem — the 5th percentile of hourly spend defines the safe commitment floor, and above that floor the algorithm must balance incremental discount gains against the probability of over-commitment waste.

followups.q: Your algorithm recommends increasing Compute Savings Plan coverage from 65% to 78% next month. Finance asks for the confidence interval on that recommendation. How do you construct it?
followups.a: Run a Monte Carlo simulation over 12 months of hourly CUR data: for each simulated month, randomly sample from the historical hourly spend distribution and compute the utilization rate at the proposed commitment level. The fraction of simulated months where utilization stays above 90% is your confidence that the new commitment will be fully utilized. For example, if 92 out of 100 simulations show >90% utilization, report '92% confidence the commitment is fully utilized, 8% probability of partial waste.' Also report the expected waste value in those 8% scenarios: average (commitment × (1-utilization_rate)) × hourly_rate × 730 hours/month. Present these as: expected annual savings, expected annual waste in downside scenarios, and net expected value. Finance understands expected value framing.

followups.q: How do you handle commitment coverage for a seasonal business where December spend is 4x January spend?
followups.a: For seasonal businesses, the floor-based algorithm must use a rolling 90th percentile of the lowest-spend months (not the annual 5th percentile) to set the stable commitment level. Commit only to the January-level floor on 1-year Compute Savings Plans. For the November-December surge, pre-purchase convertible RIs 60 days in advance for the expected peak instance families — convertible RIs can be exchanged mid-term if the peak doesn't materialize, unlike standard RIs. The cost of the 60-day convertible RI for peak season is much lower than on-demand rates and avoids the risk of a permanent over-commitment. Model the seasonal pattern explicitly: plot monthly spend for 2+ years, fit a seasonal decomposition (STL decomposition works well), and use the trend component to set the base commitment and the seasonal component to size the convertible RI buffer.

followups.q: A Savings Plan expires in 30 days and the algorithm says to renew at the same level, but the engineering team just announced a major architecture migration to Lambda. What do you do?
followups.a: First, clarify the migration timeline with the engineering lead: if the Lambda migration completes in 6 months and eliminates 40% of EC2 usage, renewing a 1-year Savings Plan at the current level creates an over-commitment 6 months in. Run the algorithm against the projected post-migration usage profile — if the team provides target Lambda invocation rates, you can estimate EC2 residual usage. Renew at 60% of the current commitment level (covering the EC2 usage you're confident survives the migration) and let the remainder convert to on-demand for now. Compute Savings Plans cover both EC2 and Lambda, so if Lambda spend grows as EC2 shrinks, the same Savings Plan applies — but validate the coverage level still makes sense for the new mix. Document the migration as an open risk in the commitment management log.

q: How do you build a unit cost trending dashboard that is actionable for engineering teams rather than just informational for finance?
a: Most unit cost dashboards fail because they show absolute spend numbers that engineers can't act on without business context. An actionable unit cost dashboard has three design principles: it pairs cost with the output metric the engineering team controls, it shows trend over time not just current period, and it surfaces the specific components driving cost changes rather than a single number. The data model starts with defining the unit: for a streaming data platform, cost per GB ingested; for a feature engineering service, cost per feature computation; for an ML serving layer, cost per 1,000 inferences. The numerator (cost) comes from your FinOps cost allocation layer, filtered to the service by namespace or tag. The denominator (volume metric) comes from your APM or business metrics system — Datadog, Prometheus, or CloudWatch. Both must use the same time aggregation window (daily or weekly) and be stored in the same warehouse for a reliable join. The dashboard layout: a primary chart showing unit cost over the last 90 days with a 7-day rolling average to smooth daily variance; a cost component breakdown (compute, data transfer, storage, external APIs) as a stacked area chart so teams can see which component is driving changes; an overlay of deployment events as vertical lines so teams can immediately correlate unit cost changes with code releases; and a 'cost driver table' showing the top 5 resource IDs that account for 80% of this service's spend, with week-over-week change. Alert integration: configure a threshold alert at 1.3x the 30-day average unit cost that routes to the team's Slack channel with a direct link to the dashboard. The goal is that when an engineer deploys a feature, they check the unit cost dashboard 24 hours later the same way they check error rate — cost becomes a standard deployment quality signal.
fj: Candidates describe unit cost dashboards as a finance deliverable — a monthly report showing cost per user that the CFO reviews.
fs: Unit cost dashboards are most valuable as real-time engineering feedback loops — engineers need to see cost per unit change within hours of a deployment, with specific cost component breakdowns and deployment event overlays, not a monthly finance summary.

followups.q: Your unit cost dashboard shows a 40% spike in cost per inference on the ML serving layer starting Tuesday. Walk me through diagnosing it.
followups.a: First, check if inference volume changed — if requests dropped 40% while cost stayed flat, unit cost rises even with no change in infrastructure. Pull both metrics from Datadog for Tuesday: if requests are flat, the issue is on the cost side. Check the cost component breakdown: if GPU compute cost is unchanged but data transfer cost jumped, look for a change in response payload size (model output vectors, embedded images). If GPU compute cost rose, check whether model serving replicas autoscaled above baseline — look at HPA events in Kubernetes. Cross-reference Tuesday with deployments: if a new model version was pushed, compare the GPU utilization rate of the old vs. new model version using DCGM Exporter metrics — a larger model may have worse throughput per GPU-hour.

followups.q: How do you choose the right unit metric for a data platform that runs both batch pipelines and interactive queries?
followups.a: Use separate unit metrics per workload type rather than one combined metric, because the production functions are different. For batch pipelines: cost per GB processed per pipeline run, tracked per named pipeline. For interactive queries: cost per query execution, segmented by user type (analyst vs. application). If you combine them into a single 'cost per active user,' a single large batch job skews the metric and hides interactive query inefficiency. Publish both metrics in a unified dashboard with separate charts but a shared time axis — this lets the team see if a unit cost spike is batch-driven or query-driven at a glance.

followups.q: The engineering team says the unit cost dashboard data is 2 days stale by the time they see it. How do you reduce the latency?
followups.a: AWS CUR data has up to 24-hour latency, which is the primary bottleneck. For near-real-time cost signals, bypass CUR and use operational metrics as cost proxies: CloudWatch billing metrics update hourly and can approximate spend by service. For Kubernetes, Kubecost's Allocation API provides cost data with 1-hour granularity using live Prometheus metrics rather than waiting for the billing export. Build a two-layer dashboard: a 'live layer' using Kubecost and CloudWatch-based cost proxies that updates hourly and provides same-day signal, and a 'reconciliation layer' using the authoritative CUR that refreshes daily and corrects for Savings Plan amortization and reserved capacity credits. Engineers use the live layer for deployment monitoring; finance uses the reconciliation layer for reporting.

q: Build a decision tree for choosing between Savings Plans and Reserved Instances for a given AWS service and workload pattern.
a: The decision tree has five decision nodes. Node 1 — Service type: if the workload runs on Fargate, Lambda, or spans multiple EC2 instance families, Compute Savings Plans are the only commitment option that covers these services — go directly to Compute SP. If the workload is EC2-only, proceed to Node 2. Node 2 — Instance family stability: will the workload stay on the same instance family for the commitment term with high confidence (>90%)? If yes and the family is large (>$5k/month spend), EC2 Instance Savings Plans give the same 72% discount as RIs with the ability to change size within the family. If the instance family may change (planned migration to Graviton, potential instance generation upgrade), use Compute Savings Plans for flexibility. Node 3 — Region pinning: will the workload stay in the same AWS region for the full term? RIs and EC2 Instance Savings Plans are region-specific by default (though EC2 Instance SPs don't require AZ pinning like standard RIs). If cross-region flexibility is needed (active-active multi-region, planned regional expansion), Compute Savings Plans are the only region-agnostic option. Node 4 — OS and tenancy: RDS Reserved Instances are the only commitment mechanism for RDS — there are no Savings Plans for RDS, Redshift, ElastiCache, or OpenSearch. For these services, the decision is always RI with no alternative. ElastiCache and OpenSearch have their own RI programs. Node 5 — Term and payment: for any commitment, select 1-year no-upfront as the default unless the workload has been running stably for 18+ months with no architecture changes planned and the organization's cost of capital is below 6%, in which case 3-year partial-upfront adds meaningful additional savings. Never buy RIs for EC2 workloads if Savings Plans are available — Savings Plans offer equal or greater discounts with more flexibility.
fj: Candidates create a single tier of recommendation — 'use Savings Plans for flexibility, RIs for maximum savings' — without recognizing that RDS, Redshift, and ElastiCache have no Savings Plan option.
fs: The most critical branch in the decision tree is service type: RDS, Redshift, ElastiCache, and OpenSearch have no Savings Plan equivalent and require service-specific Reserved Instances — applying a blanket 'use Savings Plans' policy misses these services entirely.

followups.q: A team runs RDS PostgreSQL with variable read replica count. How do you size the RDS Reserved Instance purchase?
followups.a: RDS Reserved Instances are instance-specific: you commit to a specific DB instance class (e.g., db.r6g.2xlarge), engine (PostgreSQL), and deployment type (Multi-AZ or Single-AZ). For the primary writer, which runs 24/7 at a fixed size, a 1-year RI at the exact instance class covers it fully. For read replicas that scale in and out, buy RIs only for the minimum replica count that runs continuously — if you always have at least 2 replicas running and occasionally scale to 5, buy 2 RIs and let the variable replicas 3–5 run on on-demand. RDS RI utilization is instance-class-specific, so a db.r6g.2xlarge RI cannot cover a db.r6g.xlarge replica — size the RI purchase to match your exact baseline replica configuration.

followups.q: You have both EC2 Instance Savings Plans and Compute Savings Plans active. AWS applies them in what order, and why does it matter?
followups.a: AWS applies Savings Plans in a specific priority order to maximize your savings: EC2 Instance Savings Plans are applied first to matching EC2 usage (they offer the highest discount), then Compute Savings Plans are applied to any remaining uncovered EC2, Fargate, and Lambda usage. This matters because if your EC2 Instance Savings Plan is sized too large, it may cover usage that Compute Savings Plans would have covered at a slightly lower rate, meaning you didn't need the EC2 Instance SP for that portion. The practical implication: when analyzing Savings Plans recommendations in Cost Explorer, always check whether an EC2 Instance SP recommendation is additive to your existing Compute SP coverage or redundant with it. Over-purchasing EC2 Instance SPs wastes the premium you paid for the higher discount on usage that was already covered.

followups.q: Elastic MapReduce (EMR) clusters run on EC2 — are they covered by EC2 Savings Plans or Compute Savings Plans?
followups.a: EMR clusters run on EC2 instances and are covered by both EC2 Instance Savings Plans and Compute Savings Plans, because the underlying billing is EC2 compute hours. The EMR per-instance surcharge (the EMR software fee on top of EC2) is separate and not covered by any Savings Plan — only the base EC2 instance cost gets the discount. For a cost-heavy EMR workload, Compute Savings Plans provide coverage flexibility across EMR, EKS, and on-demand EC2 without needing a specific EMR commitment product. EMR-specific cost optimization uses a separate lever — Spot Instances for task nodes and EMR managed scaling — which is independent of the RI/SP decision.

q: Design an anomaly detection threshold system for cloud billing that minimizes both false positives and missed incidents across services with different spend magnitudes and volatility profiles.
a: A single global threshold — for example, alert when any service exceeds 20% of its daily average — fails in practice because a 20% spike on a $50k/day RDS cluster is a $10k incident worth waking someone up for, while a 20% spike on a $10/day Lambda function is $2 of noise. The threshold system must be adaptive per service and calibrated to two independent dimensions: magnitude sensitivity (minimum dollar impact that justifies an alert) and statistical sensitivity (how many standard deviations from normal constitutes an anomaly). The architecture: for each service-tag combination in your cost hierarchy, compute a 28-day rolling mean and standard deviation of daily spend using the CUR data. An anomaly fires when BOTH conditions are true: (1) the dollar delta exceeds a minimum threshold (the 'materiality floor') AND (2) the deviation exceeds a z-score threshold. The materiality floor scales with service spend — use 5% of the service's 28-day average spend, with a minimum floor of $200 and a maximum floor of $10,000. This means a $100k/day EC2 service has a $5,000 materiality floor, while a $500/day Lambda service has a $200 floor. The z-score threshold should be set at 2.5 sigma for 'warning' alerts (Slack notification to team) and 3.5 sigma for 'critical' alerts (PagerDuty) — 2.5 sigma has a 1.2% false positive rate per service per day, which is acceptable for Slack noise. For services with high inherent volatility (batch pipelines that run on day 1 and day 15 of the month), replace the simple standard deviation with a seasonal-adjusted baseline: use the same day-of-month from the prior 3 months as the reference mean, reducing seasonality-driven false positives by 60–80%. Implement a feedback loop: when an engineer acknowledges an alert as 'expected' (planned deployment, scheduled batch), that acknowledgment stores a suppression window for the same service in the next 7 days and feeds the algorithm to widen the threshold for that service specifically.
fj: Candidates set a single percentage-based threshold company-wide (e.g., alert on 25% increase) and believe AWS Cost Anomaly Detection's default settings handle the rest.
fs: Effective anomaly detection requires service-specific thresholds calibrated on both dollar materiality and statistical deviation — a flat percentage threshold floods low-spend services with noise and misses critical spikes on high-spend services with naturally volatile patterns.

followups.q: Your anomaly detection system generates 40 alerts per week but engineers only act on 5. How do you tune it?
followups.a: A 12% action rate means the system has high false positive volume. Audit the 35 ignored alerts from the past month: categorize them as (a) expected seasonal patterns (batch jobs, month-end reporting), (b) below materiality threshold in actual impact, or (c) duplicate alerts for the same underlying cause. For category (a), implement seasonal suppression — compare against the same weekday 4 weeks ago rather than the 7-day rolling average. For category (b), raise the materiality floor for the affected services. For category (c), implement alert deduplication — if three child services spike simultaneously, emit one parent-level alert linking the children rather than three separate alerts. Target a 60%+ action rate as the healthy signal that the system is calibrated.

followups.q: How do you set anomaly detection thresholds for a new service that has only 2 weeks of cost history?
followups.a: Two weeks is insufficient to establish a reliable baseline. Use a three-phase cold start approach: Days 1–14, set a conservative static threshold based on the service's projected spend from the capacity plan or IaC estimate — alert on 2x the planned daily spend as an emergency-level signal. Days 15–42, transition to a low-confidence adaptive threshold using the partial history but with wider bands (3.5 sigma instead of 2.5) to reduce false positives during the learning phase. After day 42 (6 weeks of data covering at least one monthly billing cycle), switch to the full adaptive model. Tag services with their 'history age' in days so operators know which service thresholds are still in cold start mode.

followups.q: A false negative occurs — a $200k cost spike runs for 72 hours before detection. What systemic failure does this reveal and how do you fix it?
followups.a: A 72-hour miss on a $200k spike suggests the anomaly crossed the z-score threshold but fell below the materiality floor, or the service had high recent volatility that inflated the standard deviation and made the spike appear within normal range. The immediate investigation: pull the service's 28-day cost time series and mark where the spike started — if the rolling standard deviation was unusually high due to a previous spike, the adaptive threshold was inflated, making the new spike appear normal. The fix: implement a 'floor-adjusted z-score' that uses the median absolute deviation (MAD) instead of standard deviation for services with heavy-tailed cost distributions (batch jobs cause these). MAD is resistant to outliers inflating the baseline variability. Additionally, add a hard cap rule: regardless of z-score thresholds, any service with absolute daily spend exceeding $20k must have a maximum alert delay of 4 hours — achieved via a separate high-watermark alarm in CloudWatch rather than the statistical model.
